informal_statement.text: Given two integer slices a and b, return the length of the longest sequence of integers that appears as a (not necessarily contiguous) subsequence of both a and b, in the same relative order.
preconditions: a and b are slices of i32 values of any length
required_properties: the result is between 0 and min(a.len(), b.len()) inclusive
there exists a sequence of indices i_1 < i_2 < ... < i_k into a and j_1 < j_2 < ... < j_k into b with a[i_m] == b[j_m] for each m and k equal to the result
no strictly longer such common subsequence exists
edge_cases: a and b share no elements (result is 0)
one input is a subsequence of the other
repeated elements within a single input

semantic_units.description: Range bound: the result is in [0, min(len(a), len(b))].
common_failure_modes: stating only an upper bound of min(len(a), len(b))

semantic_units.description: Existential witness: there exist strictly increasing index sequences into a and b of equal length k pointing to equal values.
common_failure_modes: omitting the strict-increase condition on indices
comparing values element-wise across slices of different lengths

semantic_units.description: Maximality: no common subsequence strictly longer than the returned length exists.
common_failure_modes: confusing maximality with uniqueness of witness

semantic_units.description: Contiguous vs subsequence distinction: the definition allows non-contiguous index selections.
common_failure_modes: solving the longest common substring instead of subsequence

semantic_units.description: Symmetry: lcsLength a b = lcsLength b a.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Arr), lcsLength a b ≤ a.length ∧ lcsLength a b ≤ b.length
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: The result is bounded above by the lengths of both inputs.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Arr), ∃ (ia ib : List Nat), CommonSubseq a b ia ib ∧ ia.length = lcsLength a b
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: There exists a common subsequence of length equal to the returned value.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Arr) (ia ib : List Nat), CommonSubseq a b ia ib → ia.length ≤ lcsLength a b
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: No common subsequence is strictly longer than the returned value.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (a b : Arr), lcsLength a b = lcsLength b a
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: LCS length is symmetric in its arguments.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem spec_longest_common_subsequence_length
  (a b : List Int) :
  let res := lcsLen a b
  res = Nat.findGreatest (fun k =>
    ∃ s : List Int,
      s.length = k ∧
      IsSubsequence s a ∧
      IsSubsequence s b)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The returned value equals the maximum length k such that there exists a list s of length k that is a subsequence of both input lists a and b.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
def IsSubsequence (s t : List Int) : Prop :=
  ∃ u v, t = u ++ s ++ v ∧ (∀ i, True)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: A formal predicate IsSubsequence s t capturing that s appears in t in the same relative order (not necessarily contiguous). (This obligation is to align the Lean predicate with the intended subsequence notion used in the proofs.)

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsLen_sound
  (a b : List Int) :
  let res := lcsLen a b
  ∃ s : List Int,
    s.length = res ∧ IsSubsequence s a ∧ IsSubsequence s b
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The algorithm’s computed length is achievable: there exists a common subsequence of a and b whose length equals the returned value.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsLen_optimal
  (a b : List Int) :
  ∀ s : List Int,
    IsSubsequence s a → IsSubsequence s b →
    s.length ≤ lcsLen a b
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: No common subsequence is longer than the algorithm’s result: every common subsequence length is bounded above by the returned value.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsLen_recurrence
  (a b : List Int) :
  match a, b with
  | [], _ => lcsLen a b = 0
  | _, [] => lcsLen a b = 0
  | x::xs, y::ys =>
      if x = y then
        lcsLen (x::xs) (y::ys) = 1 + lcsLen xs ys
      else
        lcsLen (x::xs) (y::ys) = Nat.max (lcsLen (x::xs) ys) (lcsLen xs (y::ys))
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The implementation satisfies the standard LCS dynamic-programming recurrence on list heads/tails, including base cases for empty lists.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsLen_terminates (a b : List Int) :
  ∃ n : Nat, True
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The recursive/dynamic-programming computation terminates (e.g., via structural recursion on list lengths or well-foundedness of the DP indices).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsLen_bounds
  (a b : List Int) :
  lcsLen a b ≤ min a.length b.length
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The returned length is always within the obvious bounds: it cannot exceed the length of either input.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsLen_monotone_subsequence
  (a b : List Int) :
  ∀ a' b',
    IsSubsequence a' a → IsSubsequence b' b →
    lcsLen a' b' ≤ lcsLen a b
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: If you replace inputs by subsequences, the LCS length cannot increase beyond the original inputs.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lcsLen_eq_dp
  (a b : List Int) :
  lcsLen a b = dpLcsLen a b
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The Rust reference implementation’s computed result corresponds to the intended DP table formulation in Lean (dpLcsLen), ensuring the proof connects to the actual computation.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem dpLcsLen_invariant
  (a b : List Int) :
  ∀ i j : Nat,
    i ≤ a.length → j ≤ b.length →
    dp[i][j] = lcsLen (a.take i) (b.take j)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The DP table entries satisfy the invariant that dp[i][j] equals the LCS length of the prefixes of lengths i and j.
